label.text:
The McIntosh (often shortened to 'Mac') is a red-and-green apple cultivar, the national apple of Canada. It was discovered as a chance seedling by John McIntosh on his farm in Dundela, Upper Canada, in 1811, and has been propagated by grafting ever since.

The fruit has a thin, tender skin that is bright red and green when ripe, with crisp, tart white flesh that softens quickly when cooked. Its strong, slightly tangy aroma makes it a popular choice for fresh eating, applesauce, cider, and pies, though it tends to break down when baked whole.

McIntosh apples ripen in late September and are typically harvested through October. They are widely grown in Canada, the northeastern United States, and parts of Europe. The cultivar also lent its name to the Apple Macintosh computer, named in 1979 by Jef Raskin after his favourite apple variety.
